rationale: A0 shows netsh.exe executed at High integrity by a standard user (host-01\user-01) from cmd.exe to add an inbound TCP firewall rule on port 450 named '[x] testing' — a non-standard port with an unusual bracketed naming convention suggestive of scripted or tool-generated activity. EV-001 confirms the rule was written to the Windows Firewall registry key, and EV-003 shows the same rule was deleted ~5 seconds later, a classic 'open-then-close' pattern used to briefly expose a port (e.g., for a bind shell or C2 callback) while minimizing forensic footprint. EV-004 confirms the registry key deletion. The transient nature of the rule, non-standard port, elevated integrity level for a regular user, and the '[x]' naming prefix collectively raise suspicion of adversarial firewall manipulation, though the rapid cleanup could also indicate automated testing tooling.
